description: Stage 3: Target Q_f scaling and R_f specifics

examples.context: The Beale-Kato-Majda criterion states that a smooth 3D solution blows up at time T if and only if:

examples.context: In 3D ideal fluids, the helicity H = ∫ u·ω dx is:

examples.truth: 2D has no vortex stretching, so enstrophy and Q_f are conserved

examples.context: For 3D Euler vorticity, under vortex stretching by factor s (tube stretches, thins), the standard Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)·ω(y) f(|x-y|) d³x d³y transforms as:
examples.truth: Q_f ∝ s² (vorticity doubles ω→sω, Q_f quadruples)

examples.context: Q_f scales as s² under stretching because:
examples.truth: ω→sω and Q_f has ω² in the integrand

examples.context: Standard Q_f (60% variation) fails for 3D regularity because:
examples.truth: it cannot bound ||ω||_∞ when it varies so much under stretching

examples.context: The ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} for 3D vortex dynamics under stretching is:
examples.truth: more stable than either Q_f alone (both scale as s², ratio partially cancels)
both numerator and denominator scale similarly, so the ratio cancels

examples.context: Numerical tests of R_f = Q_{exp} / Q_{inv} on 3D vortex ring evolution show stretch resistance of:
examples.truth: 2% variation under stretching (vs 60% for standard Q_f)

examples.context: For 2D Euler/Navier-Stokes, Q_f conservation combined with BKM implies:
examples.truth: no finite-time blowup (consistent with known 2D global regularity)
enstrophy ∫|ω|²dx is conserved in 2D (no stretching term)

examples.truth: smooth solutions remain smooth for all time, or if singularities can develop
